summary: Graham argues that reading and experience reshape your mental models even when you forget the specific details, so the value of books isn’t measured by what you can recall. This explains why rereading matters: the same material is “compiled” differently at different times in life. He extends the idea to future tech, suggesting we may eventually replay and even edit experiences to relearn from them.
key_points: Forgetting details doesn’t erase the lasting effect reading has on your mental models.
Knowledge is like a compiled program whose source you no longer remember.
Rereading important books is valuable because your mind compiles them differently over time.
Future technologies could let us replay or edit experiences to extract new learning.
Not knowing how you know things may be contingent on current human limitations.
money_quotes: Reading and experience train your model of the world.
Your mind is like a compiled program you've lost the source of.
The same book would get compiled differently at different points in your life.
Whereas now the phrase "already read" seems almost ill-formed.
So although not knowing how you know things may seem part of being human, it may not be.
key_insight: Even when you forget what you read, it still reshapes your mental models—making rereading and re-experiencing powerful ways to deepen understanding.
